narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 38.50, also higher than the average of 36.70, you brought some impressive academic credentials to your law school application. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' bar exam outcomes, predicted a 64% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejection. This prediction hinges on just a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the decision. The biggest driver pushing toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4th, which is below the average 5.53 and signaled to the model a dip in performance relative to peers at that stage. Next, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing by standing out as better than typical. Your Year 1 decile of 6th, slightly above the average 5.71, had a milder but still negative pull, nudging the odds somewhat toward failure compared to stronger early performers. Overall, these academic deciles outweighed your other strengths, as the model prioritizes consistent high rankings across years for bar success, where students in the top deciles pass far more reliably. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile shows promise in test scores and GPA but lags in later-year consistency, which is a common stumbling block for many rejected applicants. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad-level work, and reapply after demonstrating that upward trend. You might also seek feedback from advisors on bolstering any perceived weaknesses in your transcript. With your LSAT and GPA as anchors, targeted improvements could flip this prediction next time. Keep pushing—many students in similar spots turn it around with persistence.
